text: Helix Robotics runs a fleet-control platform for warehouse robots. The backend is split into four services: fleet-api (HTTP/REST), path-planner (gRPC), telemetry-ingest (Kafka consumer), and the web dashboard. fleet-api is the only service exposed to the public internet.

path-planner is written in Rust for latency reasons; every other backend service is TypeScript on Node 20. The team rewrote path-planner from Python to Rust in 2024 after p99 planning latency exceeded 400ms under load.

Robots talk to the platform over MQTT, not HTTP. The MQTT broker is EMQX, and telemetry-ingest bridges MQTT topics into Kafka. Direct robot-to-fleet-api HTTP calls are forbidden and blocked at the network layer.

The primary datastore is PostgreSQL 16. Robot telemetry time-series data lives in TimescaleDB (a Postgres extension), while operational state (orders, zones, robot registry) lives in plain Postgres tables.

Redis is used only for ephemeral locks and rate-limiting, never as a source of truth. Anything that must survive a restart goes to Postgres. A recurring bug class at Helix is engineers caching state in Redis and assuming it persists.
All HTTP routes in fleet-api must be versioned under /v1. Breaking changes require a new version prefix; you may never change the shape of an existing /v1 response. Additive fields are allowed.
Database migrations use the `drizzle-kit` tool and live in db/migrations. Migrations are forward-only; there are no down migrations at Helix. To undo a migration you write a new forward migration.

Every service reads config from environment variables only, validated at boot with zod. If a required env var is missing the service must crash on startup, never start with a default. This is called fail-fast config and is enforced in code review.

Timestamps are always stored and transmitted in UTC as ISO-8601 strings. Local time is a display concern handled only in the web dashboard. Storing local time in the database is a blocking review comment.
Robot IDs follow the format HX-<site>-<4digits>, e.g. HX-BER-0042. The site code is a three-letter IATA-style code for the warehouse location. Robot IDs are immutable once assigned.

Feature flags are managed in the `flags` Postgres table and cached in-process for 30 seconds. There is no third-party flag provider; the team rejected LaunchDarkly in 2023 over data-residency concerns.

ADR-007 mandates that path-planner never queries Postgres directly. It receives the warehouse map via a gRPC call from fleet-api at session start and keeps it in memory. This keeps planning deterministic and DB-independent.

ADR-011 chose Kafka over RabbitMQ for telemetry because telemetry must be replayable for incident forensics. Retention is set to 14 days. RabbitMQ is still used for one thing: the email/notification queue.

ADR-014 banned ORMs for write paths. Reads may use Drizzle's query builder, but all writes go through hand-written SQL in repository classes, because an ORM auto-update once wiped a zone-config column in staging.

ADR-019 sets the deployment strategy to blue-green for fleet-api and rolling for everything else. path-planner cannot be rolling-deployed because in-memory map state would be split across versions mid-planning.

ADR-022 requires all inter-service auth to use short-lived mTLS certs issued by an internal step-ca, rotated every 24 hours. Long-lived API keys between services are forbidden; only fleet-api's public edge uses bearer tokens for customers.

path-planner is owned by Mara Voss. Any change to the planning algorithm or the gRPC schema needs her review. She is the only person who understands the cost-function weighting and is the bus-factor risk the team is actively de-risking.
telemetry-ingest and the Kafka pipeline are owned by Dev Okafor. He also owns the TimescaleDB schema and the continuous-aggregate rollups used by the dashboard charts.
The web dashboard is owned by Lin Zhao. Design-system changes go through her. The dashboard is Next.js and is the only frontend; there is no mobile app despite repeated customer requests.
Infrastructure, CI/CD, and the Kubernetes clusters are owned by Sam Reyes. Sam runs the on-call rotation and is the approver for any change to the Terraform in the infra/ repo.

Incident INC-204 (March 2025): a robot fleet in the Berlin warehouse stalled because path-planner ran out of memory holding too many concurrent session maps. Fix: a hard cap of 50 concurrent planning sessions per planner pod, plus horizontal autoscaling on memory.

Incident INC-218 (June 2025): telemetry gaps appeared because EMQX silently dropped MQTT messages above QoS 1 under backpressure. Fix: switched critical robot-state topics to QoS 2 and added a dead-letter topic. Non-critical topics stayed at QoS 0.

Incident INC-231 (August 2025): a customer's bearer token leaked in a support screenshot. Since then, fleet-api tokens are scoped per-warehouse and auto-expire after 90 days, and the dashboard redacts tokens in all UI surfaces.

Incident INC-240 (October 2025): a forward-only migration added a NOT NULL column without a default and locked the orders table for 9 minutes during deploy. New rule: NOT NULL columns must be added in two steps (nullable + backfill, then constraint).
Production runs on Kubernetes (EKS) in eu-central-1. There is no multi-region; data residency requirements keep all customer data in the EU. A US region is on the roadmap but blocked on legal review.
CI is GitHub Actions. A merge to main triggers build, test, and a blue-green deploy of fleet-api to staging. Production deploys are manual and require a second approver from the owning team.
Secrets are stored in AWS Secrets Manager and injected via the External Secrets Operator. Secrets are never committed to git; a pre-commit hook with gitleaks blocks accidental commits of credentials.

The staging environment uses a nightly anonymized snapshot of production data. Personally identifiable fields (customer names, addresses) are scrubbed by a sanitizer job before the snapshot is loaded.

fleet-api depends on path-planner (gRPC) and Postgres. If path-planner is down, fleet-api serves a degraded mode: robots already en route continue on their last plan, but no new orders can be dispatched.

The dashboard reads exclusively from fleet-api's /v1 endpoints and a websocket for live robot positions. It never queries Postgres or Kafka directly. This boundary is enforced so the dashboard can be deployed independently.

Embeddings for the semantic search over maintenance logs are computed by an internal model served via vLLM, not OpenAI, because maintenance logs contain customer-identifying robot serials and must not leave the EU cluster.

The `zones` table uses soft deletes (deleted_at column). Almost every bug in zone management traces back to a query that forgot `WHERE deleted_at IS NULL`. There is a lint rule but it only catches the obvious cases.

Robot battery percentage in telemetry is reported 0-1000 (tenths of a percent), not 0-100. Dividing by 10 for display is a step new engineers always miss, producing dashboards that show 1000% battery.

Kafka consumer group IDs must be unique per environment. A copy-paste of the staging consumer group into a local dev run once stole partitions from staging telemetry-ingest and caused a 40-minute data gap.

The path-planner gRPC deadline is 250ms. If fleet-api does not set a deadline, a slow plan can pile up requests and exhaust the connection pool. Always pass the deadline; it is not defaulted client-side.

All customer-facing endpoints require a per-warehouse bearer token; there is no global admin token in production. Internal admin actions go through a separate break-glass tool that logs to an immutable audit trail.

PII access is logged. Any read of a customer's name or address by an internal user is written to the audit_log table with the actor, reason, and timestamp. Unexplained PII access triggers a weekly review.

The orders table is the system of record for what a robot should do. An order moves through states: queued, assigned, in_progress, completed, failed. State transitions are append-only events in order_events; the orders row is a materialized projection.

Warehouse maps are versioned. Each map version has an immutable ID, and a running planning session pins a specific map version for its entire lifetime so the floor layout cannot change underneath a robot mid-task.

Telemetry is downsampled for long-term storage: raw 1Hz data is kept 14 days, 1-minute rollups for 1 year, 1-hour rollups forever. The downsampling is done by TimescaleDB continuous aggregates owned by Dev Okafor.

On-call escalation: page the primary on-call (Sam's rotation), then the owning team's lead after 15 minutes unack, then the CTO after 30. Robot-safety incidents skip straight to the CTO and trigger a fleet-wide safe-stop.

A fleet-wide safe-stop is the emergency brake: it commands every robot to halt in place via a retained MQTT message on the safety topic. Only two people can trigger it in production: Sam Reyes and Mara Voss.
